coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission carbon dots based ratiometric fluorescent sensor with opposite response for detecting copper (II)
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Gengli
Luo, Xueli
He, Xie
Han, Yong
Zhao, Haiping
Tang, Wenzhi
Yue, Tianli
Li, Zhonghong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon dots with intrinsic dual-emission fluorescence (d-CDs) at 360 nm (F360) and 530 nm (F530) under excitation of 320 nm were prepared by microwave-assisted heat treatment of o-phenylenediamine (OPD). Cu2+ could be effectively captured by the d-CDs via coordinating with the nitrogen lone pair of –NH2 to form a complex (Cu2+-OPD) by electron pair sharing. The complex can efficiently quench the fluorescence of F360 by through-bond energy transfer (TBET) and meanwhile selectively enhance the fluorescence of F530 by inhibiting the occurrence of photoinduced electron transfer (PET). The unique opposite response of the d-CDs toward Cu2+ could produce a large change to the value of F360/F530 and thus the d-CDs were utilized to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescent sensor to detect Cu2+ with a wide linear range of 0.8–55.0 μmol L−1 and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 44.63 nmol L−1 (R2 = 0.9989). Recoveries of 95.8–102.3% with relative standard deviations (RSD) < 6.7% were achieved in spiked food samples. Moreover, this sensor possessed excellent selectivity and sensitivity for the quantitative determination of Cu2+, indicating its promising potentials for constructing a reliable and convenient approach for quick pre-warning of Cu2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-emission
Ratiometric detection
Fluorescent analysis
Heavy metal

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 32060571 ], Social Development Grant of Shaanxi Province ( 2018SF-401 ), CAS "Light of West China" Program ( 2020XBZG-XBQNXZ-A ), Innovation Foundation of University in Gansu Province ( 2021A-123 ) and the Special Program for Guiding Local Science and Technology Development by the Central Government of China (2019, QNKB2-4 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the d-CDs, the inset shows the size distribution of the d-CDs; (B) UV–visible absorption and fluorescence emission spectrum of d-CDs, the insets of (a) and (b) are photos of d-CDs solution before and after under a 365 nm UV light; (C) FTIR spectra of the d-CDs before and after introduction of Cu2+; (D) XPS spectra of the d-CDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+; (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of the d-CDs at different excitation wavelength; (F) The ratio of F360/F530 of the d-CDs for 30 days at 4 °C.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the sensor versus different concentrations of Cu2+ (0.8–55.0 μmol L−1), the inset photograph presented the d-CDs solutions after adding different concentrations of Cu2+ under daylight and 365 nm UV light.); (B) The fitting curve between the intensity ratio (F360/F530) of the d-CDs based sensor and Cu2+ concentration.
The values of F360/F530 of nanosensor toward various metal ions.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of d-CDs, Cu2+ and d-CDs + Cu2+; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of the sensor toward different concentrations Cu2+; (C) Emission spectrum of d-CDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of d-CDs + Cu2+.
Schematic illustration of the d-CDs based sensor for detecting Cu2+.
Detection of Cu2+ in real samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission carbon dots based ratiometric fluorescent sensor with opposite response for detecting copper (II)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Agriculture and Forestry, Longdong University, Qingyang, Gansu 745000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Agriculture and Forestry, Longdong University, Qingyang, Gansu 745000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products (YangLing), Ministry of Agriculture, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products (YangLing)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products (YangLing), Ministry of Agriculture, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon dots with intrinsic dual-emission fluorescence (d-CDs) at 360 nm (F360) and 530 nm (F530) under excitation of 320 nm were prepared by microwave-assisted heat treatment of o-phenylenediamine (OPD). Cu2+ could be effectively captured by the d-CDs via coordinating with the nitrogen lone pair of –NH2 to form a complex (Cu2+-OPD) by electron pair sharing. The complex can efficiently quench the fluorescence of F360 by through-bond energy transfer (TBET) and meanwhile selectively enhance the fluorescence of F530 by inhibiting the occurrence of photoinduced electron transfer (PET). The unique opposite response of the d-CDs toward Cu2+ could produce a large change to the value of F360/F530 and thus the d-CDs were utilized to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescent sensor to detect Cu2+ with a wide linear range of 0.8–55.0 μmol L−1 and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 44.63 nmol L−1 (R2 = 0.9989). Recoveries of 95.8–102.3% with relative standard deviations (RSD) < 6.7% were achieved in spiked food samples. Moreover, this sensor possessed excellent selectivity and sensitivity for the quantitative determination of Cu2+, indicating its promising potentials for constructing a reliable and convenient approach for quick pre-warning of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of essential trace element for the human body, copper (II) (Cu2+) is pivotal for physical behaviors by involving the activity regulating of various proteins. Cu2+ serves as a catalytic cofactor for numerous enzymes (e.g. cytochrome c oxidase, tyrosinase, Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase, ferroxidase and lysyl oxidase) [1–4]. Therefore, insufficient intake of Cu2+ would result in some diseases related to the numbers and activity of enzymes such as hypopigmentation, oxidative damage and anaemia by affecting the activities of proteins [3]. However, an excessive intake of Cu2+ leads to its accumulation in human body and thus undermines the biological system because the redox property of copper could promote the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) which are essential in cellular metabolism [5,6]. As a result, excessive Cu2+ gives rise to severe diseases related to ROS such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's [7]. Up to date, different analytical methods such as electrochemical method [8], flame atomic absorption spectroscopy (FAAS) [9] and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10] have been developed for detecting Cu2+. These analytical techniques are limited by time-consuming, specific instrumentation and professional operators needed. Hence, the development of facile, inexpensive and convenient analytical methods of Cu2+ is imperative.

Fluorescence analysis has attracted great attention for the determination of Cu2+ for its simplicity, facile and simple equipment requirement, as well as its easy availability of luminescent nanomaterials such as metal organic frameworks, organic dyes, quantum dots, metallic nanoparticles/nanoclusters and carbon dots (CDs). CDs have gained great interest in fluorescence analysis due to their good chemical stability, excellent water solubility and easy functionalization [11]. However, the sensing performances of the fluorescent sensors by monitoring the changes of single emission intensity is easily susceptible to the matrix substances, the concentration of probe and the spectrum parameters [12], which may result in unstable fluorescent signals and thus decreasing the sensitivity and accuracy of the fluorescent sensors.

Ratiometric fluorescent sensors can decrease the inherent disadvantage of the single-emission fluorescence nanosensors due to their self-calibrations by measuring the ratio of two emission peak intensities at different wavelengths under single excitation [13]. CDs based ratiometric fluorescent sensors can be fabricated by combining two CDs with different fluorescent emitting peaks [14–16], hybridizing single-emission CDs with other fluorescent materials such as metal nanocluster [17], lanthanide coordination [18] and indirect fluorescent fluorophore [19], and designing CDs with dual emissions [20,21]. Among the above-mentioned methods, combining two CDs or hybridizing CDs with other nanoparticles required at least two or more kinds of nanoprobes, which need pre-conjugation or pre-assembly together via elaborate modifications of fluorescent materials, and complicated synthetic processes [22]. Consequently, these methods still rely on sophisticated design and ingenious assembly, which is time-consuming and arduous. Preparing CDs with dual emissions was applied to address these disadvantages due to their significantly simplifying design. Moreover, two response strategies are often presented in ratiometric sensors. For the first response strategy, one signal is target-sensitive and presents a specific fluorescence response to the target, whereas the other signal is a reference signal with a negligible response to the same target [15,16]. The second response strategy is that the ratiometric sensors possess two opposite response signals toward the target and thus can produce a large change in the ratio between two response signals compared with the first response strategy with one reference signal [23]. For the second response strategy, single kind of fluorescent material with opposite response toward targets has attracted great research interest due to their simpler, lower background noise and higher signal to background ratio when compared with the strategy that require at least two kinds fluorescent material [22]. Until now, few dual-emission CDs based ratiometric fluorescent sensors fabricated by one-pot synthesis were reported [21,24,25], especially toward the target analytes with opposite response [26]. Therefore, it is unique to develop a facile method for the simple fabrication of dual-emission CDs with opposite response for determining Cu2+.

O-phenylenediamine (OPD) was supplied by Aladdin Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HClO4, HNO3 and HCl were provided by Xilong Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Ethanol was purchase from Kelong Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China).Metal ion solutions including Cr3+, Fe3+, Al3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Li+ and Cu2+ were prepared from CrCl3, FeCl3·6H2O, AlCl3, HgCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, MgCl2, MnCl2, ZnCl2, CaCl2, NaCl, KCl, LiCl and CuCl2·2H2 O, respectively. Chemical agents used in this study were analytical reagent grade without further purification. The samples (tea, raisin and dried kiwifruit) were provided by a local supermarket.

The d-CDs were obtained with a microwave oven (Galanz, China). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were measured by Vetex70 FTIR Spectroscope (Bruker Corp, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK). Morphology of the d-CDs was observed on JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fluorescence lifetime was measured by an F7100 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra and Fluorescence spectra were obtained by a UV–vis 2550 Spectrophotometer (Japan) and a PerkinElmer LS-55 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Maryland, USA), respectively.

The d-CDs were prepared through a microwave-assisted route according to the literature with slight modifications [27]. In details, 0.4 g of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was firstly dissolved uniformly in distilled water (20 mL) to form a clear solution then was heated by a domestic microwave oven with the power of 700 W for 20 min. The resulted products (0.3 g) were dispersed in 20 mL distilled water to obtain CDs solution (15 mg mL−1). After being stood for 1 day, the solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm polyether sulfone (PES) to obtain the purified the d-CDs solution, which was stored at 4 °C for future application.

To improve sensing performance, the experimental parameters such as incubation time and incubation temperature were optimized. Under the optimum parameters, the obtained d-CDs solution was firstly diluted 50 times with ethanol to obtain d-CDs suspension (300 μg mL−1). In a typical detection of Cu2+, 1980 μL of d-CDs suspension and 20 μL of Cu2+ with varying concentrations were mixed uniformly and then placed for 7 min at room temperature (25 °C). After scanning the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F360/F530) was calculated and then used for detecting Cu2+. The selectivity of the nanosensor was also investigated by just substituting other interferential ions for Cu2+.

The samples were pretreated according to the standard methods (National food safety standard, determination of copper in food. GB 5009.13–2017, China), i.e. 0.2 g of each sample was added into the mixed solutions of 10 mL concentrated HNO3 and 0.5 mL HClO4. After being stood for overnight (12 h), the mixture was boiled until it became clear. Furthermore, the solutions were brought up to 100 mL with distilled water and then adjusted the pH to 6.5. Finally, each kiwifruit samples were sprayed with different Cu2+ standard solutions at 0, 183.75, and 367.50 μg g−1, and then detected by the d-CDs based sensor and the FAAS method.

The morphology of the d-CDs was studied by TEM and the TEM image was present in Fig. 1 A. The d-CDs have a spherical morphology with a particle size of 7.65 ± 3.31 nm. Fig. 1B showed that the d-CDs displayed two emissions centered at 360 and 530 nm when excited at 320 nm and exhibited a pale yellow fluoresce under 365 nm UV light. UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1B) of the d-CDs indicated two characteristic absorption peaks at ∼240 nm and 293 nm, which was attributed to the π-electrons in the sp2-hybridized structure of the d-CDs core [6,16] and the π–π* (aromatic ring structure) transition [23].

Fig. 1E presented the influence of excitation wavelengths on the emission spectra of the d-CDs. The d-CDs exhibited two emission peaks around 360 and 530 nm with excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 330 nm and single emission peak at 530 nm when the excitation wavelength was greater than 330 nm. The emission peak around 360 nm may account for the o-phenylenediamine core, and another emission peak at 530 nm may be derived from functional groups of the OPD on the surface of the d-CDs [20,29]. The shorter wavelength emission fluorescence peak displayed a characteristic red-shift accompanied with changed intensity when the excitation energy changed from high to low energy excitation. The fluorescent intensity of the shorter wavelength emission peak reached the highest value under the excitation of 320 nm. To date, the carbon core emission and surface state emission are often considered to be the two main fluorescence principles for CDs. The carbon core emission is excitation-dependent while the surface state emission is excitation-independent [29,30]. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime spectra of the d-CDs (Fig. S2) showed that the lifetime comprised of two parts (Table S1), and the aver lifetime varied with the emission wavelength [29]. Moreover, the d-CDs possessed abundant chemical groups such as C–OH and C=O (refer to Fig. S1) on their surface, and thus this passivated surface may result from the emission along with the carbonized core [20]. This mean that the emission peak centered at 360 nm may originate from the o-phenylenediamine structures formed in the condensation reaction of the d-CDs. In addition, the longer wavelength emission peak centered at 530 nm may be derived from the N and O related functional groups [20], which exhibited an excitation-independent behavior without apparent shift under the same conditions (refer to Fig. 1E). Generally, excitation-independent emission of CDs may resulted from their uniform edge structures and surface state [31].

Furthermore, a large emission interval of 170 nm between 360 nm and 530 nm was observed (refer to Fig. 1B), which indicated the d-CDs possessed great potential for detection applications. Moreover, the stability of the synthesized d-CDs was also investigated and presented in Fig. 1F, the value of F360/F530 presented satisfied stability for ∼30 d with slight changes (P > 0.05), which indicated their excellent stability.
The results of characterization revealed that the dual-emission CDs exhibited promising potentials for construction of ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting Cu2+.

To optimize the dual-emission CDs based sensor for Cu2+ detecting, the influence of the concentration of the d-CDs was firstly investigated in view of fluorescence intensity. As seen from Fig. S3A, the fluorescence intensity of the d-CDs at 530 nm increased as the concentrations increased. However, the fluorescence intensity of the d-CDs at 360 nm initially increased as the concentrations increased but decreased at too high concentrations, indicating that CDs cores might aggregate at higher d-CD concentrations [20]. Thus, 300 μg mL−1 was chosen as the optimal concentration for sensor constructing. Fig. S3B presented the value of F360/F530 in the different reaction time after addition of Cu2+. The value of F360/F530 conspicuously decreased with the increase of reaction time and decreased slightly after 7 min. Therefore, 7 min was considered to be the optimum reaction time. The effect of the incubation temperature (15–40 °C) on the fluorescence intensity of the d-CDs was showed in Fig. S3C. The value of ΔF360/F530 of the d-CDs remained stable at the different temperatures (P > 0.05), which indicated their excellent temperature adaptation (ΔF360/F530 means the changed value of F360/F530 of the d-CDs before and after the introduction of Cu2+). Thus, room temperature (25 °C) was chose as the optimum temperature for the following experiments.

Under optimal parameters, the influence of Cu2+ concentrations (0.8–55 μmol L−1) on the fluorescent intensity of sensor was investigated and showed in Fig. 2 A. As the Cu2+ concentration increase, the fluorescent intensity of the F360 was obviously quenched while the F530 was enhanced conspicuously. The value of F360/F530 in the different Cu2+ concentrations (0.8–55 μmol L−1) was presented in Fig. 2B. The regression equation was described as F360/F530 = −0.0378[C] + 2.43979 ([C] is the concentration of Cu2+, μmol L−1, R2 = 0.9989). The detection limit for sensor was ∼44.63 nmol L−1 according to the 3σ rule. The dual-emission the d-CDs based nanosensor possessed wider linear range and more sensitivity compared with other previous analytical sensors for detecting Cu2+ (Table S2).

Excellent selectivity is a significant factor for a nanosensor to evaluate its robustness in practical application. As seen from Fig. 3 , the value of F360/F530 decreased obviously after introducing Cu2+ of 30 μmol L−1, while it did not obviously change after adding Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Al3+ with 3-fold concentration of Cu2+ and Ni2+, Mn2+, Mg2+,Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Li+ with 10-fold concentration of Cu2+. The value of F360/F530 presented obvious difference between Cu2+ and other ions, which revealed the dual-emission based sensor possessed the excellent selectivity toward Cu2+. The high selectivity may be accounted for the high affinity of the –NH2 of the d-CDs toward Cu2+ than other metal ions and the special responding mechanism [32]. These results proved that the dual-emission CDs based nanosensor possessed high selectivity for detecting Cu2+, which could meet the requirements in practical applications.

The practical applications of the ratiometeric fluorescence sensor for detecting Cu2+ in different real samples were studied. As indicated in Table 1 , excellent results of these samples with good recovery rates ranged from 95.8 to 102.3% and relative standard deviations (RSD) < 6.7% were achieved. Moreover, the practical feasibility of the sensor was verified in comparison with FAAS and the results were showed in Table S3, where the analysis results by FAAS were consistent with the concentrations gained by the ratiometric fluorescent sensor (p > 0.05), indicating that the sensor exhibited great potential application for detecting Cu2+ in practical application.

The possible fluorescence responding mechanism was investigated. After the introduction of Cu2+, the fluorescent intensity of the nanosensor at 360 nm was obviously quenched while the fluorescent intensity at 530 nm was enhanced when excited at 320 nm (refer to Fig. 2A).

The enhancement mechanism may be induced by a static complex formation. UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu2+, d-CDs and d-CDs + Cu2+ (20 μmol L−1) were depicted in Fig. 4 A. A new absorption peak around ∼438 nm was observed (Fig. 4A) and its adsorption gradually increased with the increase of Cu2+ concentration (Fig. 4B), accompanying with a visual color change from pale yellow to orange under daylight (refer to the inset of Fig. 2A). The new absorption peak could be accounted for the formation of complexes of Cu2+ with –NH2 of OPD [23]. Furthermore, in FTIR spectra of the d-CDs (refer to Fig. 1B), the peak at 1513 cm−1 (related to –NH2) became broader after introducing Cu2+, indicating the interactions of Cu2+ with –NH2 of d-CDs. During the synthesis process of the d-CDs, the lone pairs in -NH2 of OPD on the d-CDs surface would block the surface state emission by photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Thus, the predominant enhanced mechanism could account for the inhibition of PET by forming the complex of Cu2+ with –NH2 on the d-CDs surface [23,28].

The predominant quenched mechanism of fluorescent emission peak at 360 nm could be ascribed to the through-bond energy transfer (TBET) taken place from the carbon cores of the d-CDs to the complex. The d-CDs possessed abundant residual groups of OPD on their surface, thus the formation of complex was linked with the carbon core by the rigid conjugated linker (refer to the characterization sections). Generally, the presence of a rigid conjugated linker between donor and acceptor favours the TBET process while a flexible linker assists fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process [33]. The TBET system does not require spectral overlap between donor emission and acceptor absorption, and which could afford large Stokes shifts and wavelength difference between the two emissions than those of the classical FRET system [34,35]. The sensors possessed a large Stokes shift of 210 nm and a large emission interval of 170 nm when excited at 320 nm (refer to Fig. 1B), which further demonstrated the presence of TBET process from the carbon core to the complex. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the d-CDs demonstrated partially overlap with the newly appeared UV–vis absorption peak corresponding to the complex, thus a very low energy transfer efficiency for FRET may be occurred from the core of the d-CDs to the complex [19,36]. Actually, TBET is sometimes accompanied by FRET, resulting in the failure of complete energy transfer efficiency [37,38]. Thus, the predominant fluorescence quenching mechanism of the sensor at 360 nm could be accounted for a TBET process.
In summary, the possible response mechanism for detecting Cu2+ can be concluded as the binding of Cu2+ results in inhibition of the PET process coupled TBET process.

In this work, a facile ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor was fabricated based on the dual-emission CDs prepared by using OPD as precursor via microwave-assisted heat treatment for facile and sensitive determination of Cu2+. The F360 of the d-CDs was quenched while the F530 was enhanced after introducing Cu2+. Cu2+ could coordinate with the residual groups of OPD to form the complex which could quench the F360 via TBET and selectively enhance the F360 by inhibiting PET. The special responding mechanism that was induced by Cu2+ endowed the nanosensor with high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting Cu2+ in real samples, which provided a new sight for designing dual-emission CDs based ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for practical application.

Gengli Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Xueli Luo: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xie He: Software, Supervision. Yong Han: Software. Haiping Zhao: Supervision. Wenzhi Tang: Resources. Tianli Yue: Supervision. Zhonghong Li: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 32060571], Social Development Grant of Shaanxi Province (2018SF-401), CAS "Light of West China" Program (2020XBZG-XBQNXZ-A), Innovation Foundation of University in Gansu Province (2021A-123) and the Special Program for Guiding Local Science and Technology Development by the Central Government of China (2019, QNKB2-4).
